content:
✅ Approved the contribution.
Comment: :3 wao!

simple and fun!
just note @5DN1L's comments... they're really useful !

✅ Approved the contribution.
Comment: Fun and simple clash!

Thank you for your contribution @LiSamantha! Just a minor suggestion for the problem description: For clarity, please state how to calculate the weekly savings. I would suggest removing the note (as it is somewhat vague) and adding to the second sentence: "... expenses, and weekly savings (total difference between allowances and expenses)."

Hi! 
To be confirm by @5DN1L, but I think Input section is quite confusing. Could we have something like: 
Lines 1 to 7: An integer [[allowance]] for each day of the week (Monday to Sunday).
Lines 8 to 14: An integer [[expense]] for each day of the week (Monday to Sunday).

Also, in Output, you mentionned "Total Savings", but in your tests ouput "Weekly Savings".

Stub generator
* For simplicity and clarity, please change the first 14 lines to:

loop 7 read allow:int
loop 7 read exp:int

Input
* Please change "values" to "lines" in both descriptions.

* Please use the following as the first description:

Each line contains an integer [[allow]], the daily allowance, listed in order from Monday to Sunday.

* Please rewrite the second description in a similar style.

Output
* Please use the following as the first description:

{{Total Allowance: }}, followed by the total of the daily allowances.

* Please rewrite the second and third descriptions in a similar style.

Constraints
* Please add the lower and upper bounds for [[allow]] and [[exp]], e.g.

0 ≤ [[allow]] ≤ 1000
0 ≤ [[exp]] ≤ 1000

Cases
* Currently, each pair of cases covers a similar scenario, which is good. However, the visible test mostly use larger numbers, while the validators use very small numbers. Please modify Pairs 1, 2 and 4 to use more randomised integers with a wider range of magnitudes (e.g. by using (int)(Math.random() * 1001) in Java).

Thanks.

Hello, I am new to the Codingame community, and this marks my first contribution. I have developed this puzzle as a personal milestone – it is inspired by the first program I successfully executed early in my journey as an IT learner.
 
My goal is to share a beginner-friendly challenge that introduces core programming logic and basic arithmetic, helping those just starting out build a strong foundation. While I recognize there is room for improvement, I am eager to receive feedback and suggestions from the community to refine this work. Thank you for your time and support!
I will gladly take your advice, Thank you!
Hi N3I! I've now updated the description to match your suggestion. Let me know if there’s anything else that needs adjusting, thank you!
Thank you for the feedback. I will incorporate the requested changes as soon as I return home.
Thank you for your support! I will do my best to contribute more in the future. ^_^

Hello, what do you think of this? Should I put: 'Note: This problem uses basic arithmetic. Simply sum the values of allowances and expenses, then find the difference to get the weekly savings.'
 
I think it should be labelled as "Note", since the main description should explain what the problem is about. However, perhaps I should just remove that phrase since it spoils the whole concept of the puzzle. Thank you for your time :)

I don't mind "Line 1 to 7" and "Line 8 to 14" being used instead of "First 7 lines" and "Next 7 lines", though personally, I find the latter easier to follow, since the focus is more on the number of inputs rather than the specific line numbers.

Also, if we follow @N3l's advice, where the variable names double as the descriptive text, then [[allow]] and [[exp]] would need to be revised to [[allowance]] and [[expense]] respectively in the Input and Constraints sections as well as in the stub generator.

Well I don't mind it neither, but before it was written "Line 1: Each line contains an integer..." (not just "First 7 lines", That's what I found confusing... :D Finally that's up to you LiSamantha :)

Hi! I get what you’re saying, and I totally understand why "First 7 lines" and "Next 7 lines" are easier to follow – I’ve already updated the input description to match that style. As for the instances of [[allow]], I did not change it to [[allowance]] since it was already working as intended. Thanks for walking me through these details.

Hello! I wanted to clarify that I’ve now decided to use the latter option – 'First 7 lines'. I was a bit confused about what to use since I was also referring to other completed works on this platform. Sorry for making it vague, and thank you for your support!
Perfect for me as it is now! Thanks for the updates! :)
As I mentioned above, the variable names still need to be changed.
I’ve now updated it—thank you!

There still seems to be some misunderstanding. Please make the changes below:

Input
* Use this version:

<<First 7 lines:>> An integer [[allowance]] for each day of the week (Monday to Sunday).
<<Next 7 lines:>> An integer [[expense]] for each day of the week (Monday to Sunday).

Constraints
* Please change "allow" to "allowance".

* Please change "exp" to "expense".

Stub generator
* Please change "allow" to "allowance".

* Please change "exp" to "expense".

Please feel free to ask if you have any questions! 🙂

Thanks.

Hello, I've done the update last night, please have a look if there's still something that's need to improve. Also I'll take the chance to ask question :p, I'll ask about the use of constraints, as you remember I didn't put any in that section, as I don't know what to put :D, anyways thanks for the time and effort you gave for this contribution. Highly appreciated.

Thanks for the update. I've taken a look and at the moment I don't see anything that still needs improvement 🙂

Regarding the constraints question: constraints impose limitations on the inputs and outputs of the problem (and, not applicable to this contribution, time limits for viewer-enabled puzzles). Specifically, they define the valid ranges for input values and problem scope. Such information helps coders decide things like the appropriate data types to use, the algorithmic complexity to aim for, and which edge cases they should consider while implementing a solution.

So including constraints mainly helps guide the implementation and ensures that solutions are designed with the expected limits in mind.

Thanks again for the effort you've put into this contribution, and don't hesitate to ask again if you want!

No problem. Here are my comments on the updated contribution:

Output
* Please restore the line numbers before each description, similar to the Input section.

Cases
* Zero Values (Test 2): Please adjust the case so that the weekly saving is 0, while keeping some allowance values and some expense values at 0.

* Zero Savings (Validator 2): Please adjust the case so that some allowance values and some expense values are 0, while maintaining the weekly saving of 0.

* Negative Savings (Test 4): Please modify some of the input values so they are 1-digit and 2-digit integers.

* Overspent (Validator 4): Please modify some of the input values so they are 1-digit and 2-digit integers.

Thanks.

Hi there! Thanks so much for the feedback on my first puzzle contribution – I really appreciate the guidance as I’m getting started here. I’ve now made all the adjustments you suggested, and everything’s been updated to match the requirements. Let me know if you have any other suggestions or if there’s anything else I should tweak!

Just one more comment from me:

Cases
* Zero Savings (Validator 2): Please revise the allowances so that at least one value is 0. For example, you could change the first 500 to 800, and the first 300 to 0.

Thanks.
